items.title: Freeze parameters at inference
items.avoid: Leaving model.train() enabled or calling backward() in serving code paths.
items.prefer: Explicit eval mode, no_grad context, and immutable checkpoints loaded per deployment version.
items.why: Inference must be fast, deterministic, and free of accidental θ updates.
items.example: Serving handler: model.eval(); preds = model(x) inside torch.no_grad() — never opt.step().

items.title: Version and bundle train artifacts
items.avoid: Deploying only weight files without the scaler, encoder, or feature list used during fit().
items.prefer: Ship a model bundle (weights + preprocessing + metadata) so predict() receives the same x representation as fit().
items.why: Training–serving skew is a top cause of silent inference failures in production.
items.example: Save joblib.dump({'model': clf, 'scaler': scaler}) and load both in the API before predict().

items.title: Separate training and serving pipelines
items.avoid: Sharing one code path that sometimes fits and sometimes predicts based on runtime flags in production.
items.prefer: Dedicated offline training jobs and a thin inference service that only loads checkpoints and runs forward passes.
items.why: Clear boundaries prevent accidental fit on live data and simplify scaling each phase independently.
items.example: Airflow DAG runs nightly fit on labeled warehouse table; Kubernetes deployment loads new artifact and serves predict only.

items.title: Benchmark inference separately from training
items.avoid: Assuming training loss convergence guarantees acceptable p99 latency at production QPS.
items.prefer: Load-test predict latency with realistic batch sizes, hardware, and concurrency before launch.
items.why: A model that trains well may be too large or slow for real-time inference constraints.
items.example: Profile 100 concurrent predict calls on the serving CPU tier; target p99 < 100 ms before shipping.
